preFix.title: Rib of beef on the bone
preFix.subtitle: Two to three ribs, high heat, medium-rare
preFix.excerpt: A bone-in rib of beef seasoned with salt and roasted at high heat for a deep crust, then finished at moderate heat until medium-rare throughout. The most impressive of all British roasting joints.
preFix.sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Roast rib of beef, the centrepiece of the British Sunday roast.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845). Rib roasting methods.

content.text: A bone-in rib of beef is the most celebrated of all British roasting joints. The bones act as a rack, keeping the meat off the pan and allowing heat to circulate beneath it. The fat cap on top bastes the meat as it renders. The only decisions required are how you want the beef cooked and whether to use a thermometer.

Rare: 52 degrees C. Medium-rare: 55-58 degrees C. Medium: 60-65 degrees C. Well done: above 70 degrees C. A rib of beef is best at medium-rare. Remove from the oven 5 degrees C below your target: the temperature rises during resting.

Remove the beef from the fridge 2 hours before cooking. Heat the oven to 220 degrees C fan. Pat the joint completely dry. Mix the salt and mustard powder (if using) together and rub all over the joint. Rub with oil.
Place bone-side down in a roasting tin.
in the oven at 220 degrees C for 20 minutes to form a deep crust. Reduce to 180 degrees C fan and continue roasting for 12 to 15 minutes per kg for medium-rare.
Check with a thermometer: remove at 52 to 53 degrees C for medium-rare (it will rise to 55 to 58 degrees C during resting). Transfer to a warm board.
for 30 minutes under a tent of foil. To carve, run the knife along the rib bones to remove them as a slab, then slice across the grain.

items.fix: Always bring beef to room temperature for 2 hours. A cold joint needs 20 to 30 minutes extra. Trust the thermometer over the timer.
items.cause: The joint was very cold when it went in, or the oven temperature dropped when the door was opened.
items.symptom: The outside is dark but the inside is still rare after the calculated time

items.fix: Pat the beef completely dry before going into the oven. A dry surface forms a crust; a damp surface steams.
items.cause: The surface was not completely dry, or the blast time was not long enough.
items.symptom: The crust is pale and soft despite the initial blast

items.fix: Check the temperature in the thickest part of the meat, at least 2 cm from any bone. Bones conduct heat and read higher than the meat itself.
items.cause: It was left in too long, or the thermometer was placed too close to the bone.
items.symptom: The joint is overcooked and grey throughout

items.fix: Add 100 ml of water to the base of the tin before the blast and top up as needed. The pan juices are valuable for gravy.
items.cause: The fat rendered and dripped onto the pan at high heat during the blast.
items.symptom: The pan juices have burnt to the base of the tin

content.text: Season with just salt for the simplest version, which shows the quality of the beef most clearly. A coating of horseradish and breadcrumbs on the fat cap gives a textured crust. The pan juices make the base of the red wine gravy recipe.

The rib of beef on the bone is arguably the most important dish in British cooking history. It gave the British the nickname rosbif from the French. It was celebrated in eighteenth-century verse and painting as a symbol of British character and prosperity. Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton describes it as the centrepiece of the British Sunday table, a position it has never entirely surrendered.
